AI Engineering Conference

Controlling the
Uncontrollable

Lessons from building a production AI development workflow â€” and what GitHub Copilot actually does when you try to use it seriously.

ADAM SMOLOWICZ

DIVBYZERO CONSULTING AB

02 / 24
The team

Four roles. One human.

Authority
Product Owner
Human. Decides what gets built. Approves every merge. The final word.
Architecture + Review
Tech Lead â€” AI
Reviews all PRs. Makes technical decisions. Produces verdicts and fix prompts.
Implementation
Developer â€” AI
Opens PRs. Posts dumps. Waits for instruction. Never acts autonomously.
Heuristic analyser
GitHub Copilot
Automated first-pass reviewer. Advisory. Cannot gate merges.

The human is the only one who merges. The AIs do everything else.

03 / 19
Problem 1 â€” Re-review

The first review works.

PR opened
â†’
Copilot reviews
â†’
Findings appear
â†’
Fix pushed
â†’
Fix pushed

On a multi-iteration workflow â€” where a PR might go through four fix rounds before merge â€” the first Copilot review is genuinely useful. Style violations, test gaps, cross-file inconsistencies visible in the diff. Real findings.

The second review is the problem.

04 / 19
Problem 1 â€” Re-review

"Review new pushes" doesn't.

PR opened
â†’
Copilot reviews âœ“
â†’
Fix pushed â€” ?
â†’
Fix pushed â€” ?

We tried everything documented. REST API, GraphQL, GitHub CLI. All work for the initial request. All fail after the first review â€” 422, silent no-ops, nothing.

Community reports

Failure to re-review on subsequent pushes affects roughly 1-in-3 PRs even with "Review new pushes" enabled. The mechanism is undocumented.

The only reliable path: click "Re-request review" in the GitHub UI. Not acceptable in an automated workflow.

05 / 19
Problem 1 â€” Re-review

What we observed

A documentation-only push between two source code pushes consistently triggered re-review on the subsequent source push â€” even when a direct source-to-source push did not.

Two consistent data points. Not yet confirmed as a reliable pattern.

The hypothesis

Copilot tracks an internal baseline of its last reviewed commit. A documentation push shifts the baseline without triggering a review. The next source push lands as genuinely new content.

src push â†’ âœ“ Copilot fires
src push â†’ âœ— Copilot silent
docs push â†’ (no review)
src push â†’ âœ“ Copilot fires

Posted to GitHub community forum. Investigation ongoing.

06 / 19
Before

Event-driven

Run on every push

5Ã— reviews per PR. Credits drain. Noise increases. Re-review unreliable.

After

Interrupt-driven

Run when a human decides it adds value

1â€“2 deliberate reviews per PR. Signal improves. Credits controlled.

07 / 19
Solution

Label-triggered review

Add the ai-review label. One review fires. Label is automatically removed.

Concurrency block prevents parallel runs. Cleanup runs even on failure.

"Review new pushes" disabled.

# .github/workflows/copilot-review.yml

name: Copilot PR Review (on label)

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled]

jobs:
  copilot-review:
    if: ${{ github.event.label.name == 'ai-review' }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write

    concurrency:
      group: ai-review-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
      cancel-in-progress: false

    steps:
      - name: Request Copilot review
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          --add-reviewer @copilot --repo ${{ github.repository }}

      - name: Remove ai-review label
        if: always()  # runs even on failure
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: gh pr edit ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          --remove-label ai-review --repo ${{ github.repository }}
08 / 19
Solution â€” label lifecycle

The label is a contract, not a convention.

  • Do not add the label if already present on the PR
  • Do not add the label while a run is already in progress
  • Do not add the label unless a new run will produce meaningfully different input
  • At most one Copilot review per review phase (initial or pre-merge)
  • Re-review only after changes affecting program behaviour â€” logic, interfaces, data flow
  • If re-review fails: do not retry. Escalate to full-file human review.

Result: ~70â€“80% credit reduction vs "Review new pushes". Signal-to-noise ratio improves significantly.

09 / 19
Result
70%
Credit reduction vs event-driven model
1â€“2
Deliberate reviews per PR
instead of 5+ automatic ones

The real gain

When you run Copilot five times on a PR that iterates five times, at least three of those runs review code that has already been fixed. The findings are noise.

When you run it twice â€” deliberately, at moments where the review matters â€” the findings are worth reading.

The credit saving is real. The signal improvement is more important.

10 / 19
Problem 2

Copilot is not a reviewer.

It reviews the PR diff. It does not review the codebase.
It cannot detect inconsistencies in code that is not part of the diff â€” even if the change logically affects it.

11 / 19
Problem 2 â€” diff-bounded review

A real example.

The incident

A strategy interface change was made in session.py. Copilot reviewed the file correctly. Found nothing wrong.

A dependent file â€” game.py â€” still called the old interface. It was not in the diff. Copilot did not flag it.

The bug would have shipped. The tech lead's cross-file consistency check caught it.

Copilot's review was accurate within its scope.

Its scope was too narrow.

12 / 19
Solution â€” review architecture

Three layers. Each covers what the others miss.

1
Copilot â€” heuristic analyser
Fast. Shallow. Diff-bounded. Advisory. Catches what it can see.
2
Tech lead â€” deep review with full context
Cross-file consistency. Interface impact. Fetches files not in the diff for interface changes and multi-file PRs.
3
Process â€” controls when each layer runs
Label lifecycle, review phases, depth policy. Prevents automation theatre.

Layer 2 can be a human using Copilot Chat for cross-file questions â€” the process is identical.

13 / 19
Problem 3 â€” multi-agent coordination

The hard stop rule.

After posting the PR dump, the developer stops completely. It does not read the reviewer's comments. It does not push fixes. It does not act on anything it observes. It waits.

Every iteration is gated by the tech lead. Nothing autonomous happens between the dump and the verdict.

14 / 19
Problem 3 â€” multi-agent coordination

Why the rule exists.

PR #58 â€” the incident that created the rule

The developer agent observed Copilot's comments, interpreted them, and pushed what it thought were fixes. Seven iterations between developer and Copilot without tech lead involvement. The fixes introduced a subtle spec change that was not requested. Invisible until three PRs later.

An AI agent that acts on its own interpretation of reviewer comments â€” without human oversight â€” introduces spec drift at a rate that compounds silently.

The constraint feels slow. The alternative is slower.

15 / 19
What this produced

The process is the product.

Every failure produced a process change. Every process change was documented in a PR. The PR history is the failure history.

When the process breaks, it gets fixed in a PR.

What we shipped

Test coverage 98.8%
Tests 247
PRs merged 102
Documented failure modes 4

Teams that use AI well treat AI failures as process failures â€” and fix them.

16 / 19
What we actually fixed

What we had wasn't a broken system.

It was an ambiguous one.

17 / 23

You didn't know what silence meant.

And once you don't know what silence means â€”

you stop thinking about the work.

You start thinking about the system.

What ambiguity does to you

Who performs well feeling like this?

19 / 23

The Unsettling Silence

...what it does to you

confident calm focused trusted motivated energised clear engaged flowing assured present performing

When a team feels like this consistently â€” that is team health.

20 / 23

Teams that can focus on the right things
are the teams that are likely to perform.

21 / 23
Soâ€¦ did this actually create happier teams? Yes. But not in the way I expected. We didn't run workshops. We didn't talk about culture. We didn't try to "improve team health." We just removed the things that made work harder than it needed to be. People stopped checking things they shouldn't have to check. They stopped compensating. They justâ€¦ worked. My leading star doesn't tell me what to do to maximize customer value. It tells me what I can do today so the team actually functions well. Because a healthy team is far more likely to perform. And performance is what creates customer value.
